title: IMPT — Índice Multidimensional e Multimodal de Pobreza nos Transportes
text:
1 Introduction
This document describes the methodology behind the Índice Multimodal de Pobreza nos Transportes (IMPT) — a composite, multimodal transport poverty index developed for the Lisbon Metropolitan Area (Área Metropolitana de Lisboa, AML) by the U-Shift Lab at Instituto Superior Técnico. This research id funded by PLANAPP - Centro de Planeamento e de Avaliação de Políticas Públicas, in collaboration with the Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia, under the Science4Policy (S4P): Concurso de Estudos de Ciência para as Políticas Públicas call.
The IMPT is designed to quantify the degree to which residents in different locations across the AML are disadvantaged in terms of access, mobility, affordability, and safety across multiple transport modes (car, public transit, cycling, walking). It is intended to be reproducible and replicable for other metropolitan areas in Portugal.
The index covers 124 parishes (freguesias, or districts) and 18 municipalities (municípios) in the Grande Lisboa and Península de Setúbal NUTS-II sub-regions, corresponding to approximately 2.87 million inhabitants (Census 2021).

title: IMPT — Índice Multidimensional e Multimodal de Pobreza nos Transportes
text:
1.1 Study Area
The study area corresponds to the Lisbon Metropolitan Area (AML), defined as the combination of the NUTS-III sub-regions Grande Lisboa and Península de Setúbal, using administrative boundaries from CAOP 2024.1 (Carta Administrativa Oficial de Portugal, Direção-Geral do Território).
The geographic areas used throughout the pipeline include three nested levels:

Municipalities (municípios): 18 units
Parishes (freguesias): 124 units (post-2013 and 2024 reform boundaries, updated to CAOP 2024)

H3 Hexagonal Grid, Resolution 8 (primary unit for routing calculations): 3,686 hexagons, edge length ≈ 531 m, average area ≈ 0.74 km². This is a universal grid.







Administrative boundary update



Several parishes changed between the 2016 IMOB travel survey, the Census 2021, and the CAOP 2024 boundaries. A conversion table and area-weighted redistribution procedure was applied to ensure consistency (see Section 3.2).

title: IMPT — Índice Multidimensional e Multimodal de Pobreza nos Transportes
text:
1.2 Conceptual Framework
The IMPT is structured around four dimensions, assessed across four transport modes:



Table 1.1: IMPT dimensional structure





Dimension
Modes assessed




Accessibility
Car, PT1, Cycling, Walking


Mobility
Car, PT, Cycling, Walking


Affordability
Car, PT


Safety
Car, Cycling, Walking






Each dimension is computed as a composite of multiple indicators, normalised to a [1–100] scale, and then aggregated using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to produce a single dimensional score. An overall IMPT score is derived as the geometric mean (or entropy-weighted product) of the four dimension scores.
The next sections describe the data preparation, the calculation of each indicator, the PCA-based dimensional scores, and the final index construction.

title: IMPT — Índice Multidimensional e Multimodal de Pobreza nos Transportes
text: PT: Public Transport↩︎

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.1 Infrastructure and Helper Functions
00a_impt_data_handle.R defines two core helper functions:
These functions abstract away the data storage location, making the pipeline portable.
00b_data_load.R sets up globally-used variables (study area boundaries, grid, ids correspondence between levels, etc.) that are reused across scripts.

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
impt_read(path): reads CSV, GeoPackage, GeoJSON, or RDS files. When running on the ushift-alfa server, it reads from the local /data/IMPT/ directory; on any other machine, it fetches via HTTPS from https://impt.server.ushift.pt using an API key.
impt_write(content, path): writes to the same location logic (server vs. local data/ folder).

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.2 Administrative Boundaries
Source: CAOP 2024.1 (DGT — Direção-Geral do Território)
The full continental Portugal polygon file is filtered to the Grande Lisboa and Península de Setúbal NUTS-II sub-regions. For the municipality of Lisboa, parish boundaries with the Tagus estuary are replaced by clipped geometries (FREGUESIASgeo.gpkg - using CAOP 2016 parish limits) to remove the water area in Lisbon municipality.
For parishes with multiple polygon features (due to small islands), only the polygon with the largest area is retained.

3.2.1 Conversion of 2016 to 2024 Parish Codes
The Census 2021 and IMOB 2017 travel survey use 2016 parish codes (DICOFRE). Several parishes were split between 2016 and 2024. A manual conversion table was created after inspecting the differences:



Table 3.1: Parish code conversions from 2016 to 2024





Old DICOFRE (2016)
New DICOFRE(s) (2024)




151007
151008, 151009, 151010


111127
111134, 111135


111123
111129, 111131, 111132


111126
111130, 111133






Trips associated with split parishes were redistributed proportionally to the area of the new parishes. Conservation of total trip counts was validated using assertthat.

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.3 H3 Hexagonal Grid






flowchart LR
    AML["AML boundary<br/>CAOP 2024"] --> H3["h3jsr::polygon_to_cells<br/>Resolution 8"]
    H3 --> Cells["3,686 hexagons<br/>531m edge, 0.74 km2 avg"]
    Cells --> C["Grid centroids<br/>for routing"]
    Cells --> G["Grid index table<br/>mapping levels"]




Figure 3.1: H3 grid generation and spatial assignment





The H3 hexagonal grid is generated at Resolution 8 using the h3jsr package (Uber Technologies 2018). Resolution 8 provides a spatial unit with an average edge length of ~531 m and average area of ~0.74 km², which offers a good balance between spatial detail and computational tractability for routing-based accessibility measures.
The H3 grid covers the AML with 3,686 active hexagons (those intersecting the administrative boundaries). At the limits, if a hexagon covers less than 50% of the administrative area, it is excluded from the analysis. Each hexagon is assigned to a parish and municipality via a cross-reference table (grid_nuts.csv).

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.4 Census Data Processing
Source: INE Census 2021, BGRI 2021 at the base statistical unit level (BGRI — Base Geográfica de Referenciação de Informação), NUTS-3 Área Metropolitana de Lisboa (code 170).
Census BGRI polygons are converted to centroids. The following variables are extracted:



Table 3.2: Census variables used in IMPT










Variable
INE field
Description




population
N_INDIVIDUOS
Total residents


households
N_NUCLEOS_FAMILIARES
Total households


youth
N_INDIVIDUOS_0_14
Residents aged 0–14


adults
N_INDIVIDUOS_15_24 + N_INDIVIDUOS_25_64
Residents aged 15–64


elderly
N_INDIVIDUOS_65_OU_MAIS
Residents aged 65+


women
N_INDIVIDUOS_M
Female residents


buildings
N_EDIFICIOS_CLASSICOS
Classic buildings


buildings_pre1945
N_EDIFICIOS_CONSTR_ANTES_1945
Buildings built before 1945






For parish- and municipality-level aggregation, census data from 2016-era parishes are redistributed to 2024 parishes using the area-weighted conversion table described in Section 3.2.1.

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.5 Dasymetric Grid Population Redistribution (COS 2023)
Source: COS 2023 v1 (Direção-Geral do Território), residential land use classes
To achieve a more accurate population distribution within the H3 grid, a dasymetric mapping approach is applied using the Carta de Ocupação do Solo (COS) 2023. This is a relevan method, in particular when dealing with BGRI polygons that cover large areas, accross multiple hexagons cells - preventing that one single hexagon is assigned the entire population of the BGRI polygon with it’s centroid.
Residential areas are extracted with the following classes and assigned weighting factors:



Table 3.3: COS 2023 residential classes and dasymetric weights





COS 2023 classification
Weight




Contínuas predominantemente verticais
3.0


Contínuas predominantemente horizontais
1.0


Descontínuas
0.6


Descontínuas esparsas
0.3






The procedure (02_census_grid_with_cos.R):

Census BGRI polygons are intersected with COS residential patches and H3 grid cells.
For each BGRI fragment, a weighted score is computed as fragment_area × weight.
Each BGRI’s population is distributed proportionally to the weighted score of each fragment.
Results are summed per H3 cell.

This recovers approximately 99.4% of the total AML population (2,854,660 vs. 2,870,208 in the census), a significant improvement over simple centroid assignment.






Important



The land use and census data from the dasymetric method is only used at the grid level for the calculation of fine-grain indicators. For the other levels, the Census 2021 BGRI data is used directly.

title: 3  Data Preparation
section: 3.7 Travel Survey Data and OD Jittering
text:
3.7 Travel Survey Data and OD Jittering
Source: IMOB 2017/18 — Inquérito à Mobilidade nas Áreas Metropolitanas do Porto e de Lisboa (INE / AMT). https://www.ine.pt/xurl/pub/349495406
The IMOB survey served as base to produce the OD matrix at parish level with modal split (walk, bike, car, public transit, other), available at biclaR project.

3.7.1 Jobs as a proxy
Since high-resolution employment data was not available (or open) for the AML during the report development, employment locations were proxied using the jittered destinations of trips from the IMOB 2017 travel survey. Specifically, trips with the purpose “Ir para o trabalho” (Commute to work) and “Tratar de assuntos profissionais” (Work-related business) were extracted, and their jittered destination points were used as POIs representing the spatial distribution of jobs.
Since accessibility and cost computations require disaggregated spatial locations, jittering is applied to generate point-level OD pairs.


3.7.2 Jittering Algorithm
Jittering is performed using the odjitter R package (Lovelace and Félix 2022), following the approach described in Lovelace, Félix, and Carlino (2022).
The algorithm disaggregates parish-level trip counts into point-to-point flows. The following layers and weights are used:

Origins: Randomly sampled points within residential buildings.
Destinations: Sampled from a building layer (from GDA), weighted by building volume (estimated as height * footprint_m2). This ensures that areas with higher office and industrial density attract more jittered trips.
Threshold: A disaggregation threshold of 50 trips is applied, meaning any OD flow with more than 50 trips is split into multiple point-to-point pairs to provide higher spatial resolution.
Deterministic Randomization: A fixed seed (rng_seed = 42) is used to ensure the generated points are consistent across pipeline runs.

The process generates approximately 18,500 jittered OD pairs representing the daily commuting pattern in the AML. This is then expanded to the real number of trips using the PESOFIN weights.

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.8 Road Network and DEM
Road network: Exported from HOT Export Tool in .pbf format for use with r5r. The network includes all OSM road types and GTFS transit feeds.
Elevation: Digital Elevation Model at 30 m resolution downloaded from Copernicus ESA (Copernicus DEM GLO-30). Used to account for topographic effects on walking and cycling travel times via the Minetti metabolic-equivalent cost function.
The r5r network is built using r5r::build_network() with the elevation = "MINETTI" option.

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.9 Modal Share from Census
Source: Census 2021 — question on mode used for the daily commute.
Script 02_census_modalShare.R extracts and computes modal shares (private vehicle, public transit, active modes) at parish and municipality level. These are used to compute modal-share-weighted affordability metrics (see Section 5.3).

title: 3  Data Preparation
text:
3.10 Vehicle Ownership
Source: IMOB 2017 data (02_veh_ownership.R).
Vehicle ownership variables include average motorised vehicles per household and percentage of households without a vehicle, aggregated at parish and municipality level.




Lovelace, Robin, and Rosa Félix. 2022. “Odjitter: Jitter Origin-Destination Data.” https://github.com/itsleeds/odjitter.


Lovelace, Robin, Rosa Félix, and Dustin Carlino. 2022. “Jittering: A Computationally Efficient Method for Generating Realistic Route Networks from Origin-Destination Data.” Findings. https://doi.org/10.32866/001c.33873.


Uber Technologies. 2018. “H3: A Hexagonal Hierarchical Spatial Index.” https://h3geo.org.

title: 4  Transport Routing — Travel Time Matrix
text:
Script: 03_ttm_gridh3.R
Package: r5r (Pereira et al. 2021)
A travel time matrix (TTM) is computed between all H3 grid centroids for the AML, for each transport mode, using r5r::travel_time_matrix(). The parameters used are:




Table 4.1: Travel time matrix parameters






Parameter
Value




Spatial unit
H3 Resolution 8 centroids (~3,686 origins)


Percentile
50th percentile (median)


Max trip duration
120 minutes


Modes
Walk, Cycling, Car, Transit (×2 transfer scenarios)


PT egress mode
WALK


PT max walking time
15 minutes


PT departure datetime
Wednesday 04/02/2026 at 08:00 AM


PT max transfers (scenario 1)
max_rides = 2 (1 transfer)


PT max transfers (scenario 2)
max_rides = 3 (2 transfers)


Cycling max LTS
LTS ≤ 3 (comfortable streets)


Elevation model
MINETTI metabolic-equivalent
















Note



The 50th percentile travel time is used so that results represent typical (median) travel conditions rather than best- or worst-case scenarios. All parameters not listed here use r5r defaults.






Pereira, Rafael H. M., Marcus Saraiva, Daniel Herszenhut, Carlos K. V. Braga, and Matthew Wigginton Conway. 2021. “R5r: Rapid Realistic Routing on Multimodal Transport Networks with R5 in R.” Findings. https://doi.org/10.32866/001c.21262.
crumbs: 4  Transport Routing — Travel Time Matrix

title: 5  Dimension Calculations
text:
5.1 Accessibility
Script: 04_access_opportunities.R
Package: accessibility (Pereira and Herszenhut 2022)
flowchart TB
    TTM["Travel Time Matrix<br/>5 modes × 2 transfer scenarios"] --> CumCut
    POIs["POIs per H3 cell<br/>(13 categories, spatially joined)"] --> CumCut
    Census["Population per H3 cell<br/>(residents, kids, elder, active)"] --> CumCut
    
    subgraph CumCut["Cumulative Accessibility"]
        direction TB
        Loop1["For each POI category"] --> Loop2["For each transport mode"]
        Loop2 --> Loop3["For each time cutoff<br/>5,10,15,30,45,60,75,90 min"]
        Loop3 --> Calc["accessibility::cumulative_cutoff()<br/>Count opportunities reachable<br/>within cutoff time"]
    end
    
    CumCut --> Agg["Aggregate to parish/municipality<br/>(weighted mean by population segment)"]
    Agg --> Out["Output: access_{poi}_{mode}_{time}_{census}"]




Figure 5.1: Accessibility computation flowchart

title: 5  Dimension Calculations
text:
5.1.1 Metric naming convention
Accessibility metrics follow the pattern:
access_{poi_type}_{mode}_{time}_{census_group}
For example: access_health_pt_30min_residents = number of health facilities reachable by public transit within 30 minutes, for the total resident population.


5.1.2 Aggregation to parish and municipality level
Grid-level accessibility values are aggregated to parishes and municipalities using a population-weighted mean, separately for each demographic group (residents, kids, active, elderly).


5.1.3 Time to closest opportunity (mobility costs)
Script: 04_mobility_costs_time.R
Function: accessibility::cost_to_closest()
The travel time to the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd closest opportunity of each POI category is also computed, allowing measurement of baseline access time for the nearest facilities.

title: 5  Dimension Calculations
text:
5.2 Mobility
Mobility indicators cover infrastructure availability and quality, public transport coverage and service levels, commuting patterns, and shared mobility provision.

5.2.1 Walking infrastructure ratio
Script: 04_mobility.R
The pedestrian infrastructure ratio is the proportion of road length that includes pedestrian infrastructure, computed using OSM data:
\[R_{ped} = \frac{\text{pedestrian path length}}{\text{road length}}\]
Road classification uses highway tags: motorway, trunk, primary, secondary, tertiary, unclassified, residential, living_street, plus link variants.
Pedestrian infrastructure uses tags: highway = footway | pedestrian | steps | residential and footway = sidewalk | crossing | path | platform | corridor | alley | track, and sidewalk = both | left | right.


5.2.2 Cycling infrastructure ratio and quality
Script: 04_mobility.R
The cycling infrastructure ratio is computed analogously to the pedestrian ratio. Cycling infrastructure data comes from the classified cycle network layer AML_cycle_class.gpkg (TML PMUS 2024), which categorises cycleways into:

Segregated cycleway (ciclovia segregada)
Painted cycle lane (ciclofaixa pintada na via)
Shared pedestrian infrastructure (infraestrutura pedonal partilhada)
Shared road with vehicles, signed (via partilhada com veículos motorizados, sinalizada)

The cycling quality ratio is: \[R_{cyc,q} = \frac{\text{segregated cycleway length}}{\text{total cycleway length}}\]


5.2.3 PT stop coverage
Script: 04_mobility.R / 04_mobility_transit.R
The proportion of population within 500 m of a public transport stop is computed by:

Creating a 500 m buffer around every PT stop in the AML (all operators, from GTFS).
Counting BGRI centroids whose population falls within these buffers.
Dividing by total parish population.

Limitation: This is a binary coverage measure (served/not served); it does not account for service frequency or the number of modes available at each stop.


5.2.4 PT headways and waiting times
Script: 04_mobility_transit.R
Package: tidytransit
Stop-level service frequencies are computed with tidytransit::get_stop_frequency() for four time windows:



Table 5.1: PT service frequency periods





Period
Date
Time window




Weekday peak
Wed 04/02/2026
08:00–09:00


Weekday full day
Wed 04/02/2026
00:00–48:00


Weekday night
Wed 04/02/2026
22:00–23:00


Weekend
Sun 08/02/2026
10:00–11:00






A 500 m buffer per stop is used to allocate stops to census population centroids. Results are aggregated to parishes and municipalities as population-weighted mean headway and waiting time.
Additionally, frequency reduction factors are computed to characterise night and weekend service reduction.


5.2.5 Commuting travel times
Script: 04_mobility_commuting.R
Using the jittered IMOB OD pairs and the travel time matrices, average commuting travel times are computed for each geographical unit and mode, weighted by number of trips. Trips with no feasible route within the constraints are assigned the maximum travel time (120 min) and counted separately as “not accomplishable” trips (PNA — Percentagem Não Alcançáveis).






flowchart TB
    TTM["Travel Time Matrix<br/>(all modes)"] --> Match
    ODs["Jittered OD pairs<br/>(IMOB 2017 commuting)"] --> Match
    Grid["H3 Grid"] --> Match
    
    subgraph Match["Spatial Assignment & Matching"]
        direction TB
        JoinGrid["Assign each OD<br/>to origin grid cell"] --> Lookup["Look up travel time<br/>for each OD pair<br/>in TTM"]
        Lookup --> Fill["Fill missing:<br/>max_duration = 120 min<br/>(NAs = not accomplishable)"]
    end
    
    Match --> AggMode["Aggregate by<br/>geographic unit × mode"]
    AggMode --> Output["avg_tt_{mode}<br/>trips, trips_na_{mode}<br/>PNA_{mode}"]




Figure 5.2: Commuting travel time computation







5.2.6 Number of PT transfers
Script: 04_mobility_commuting.R (line ~181)
For each commuting OD pair, the minimum number of transfers required is determined by checking whether a route exists in the matrices computed for 0, 1, 2, and 3 transfers. The minimum number of transfers for which a route exists is assigned to each OD pair, and population-weighted mean transfers are computed per geographical unit.


5.2.7 Shared mobility
Source: TML PMUS (Shared mobility dock locations). https://pmmus.tmlmobilidade.pt/
The number of shared mobility points (physical and virtual docks) is counted per parish and per H3 cell.

title: 5  Dimension Calculations
text:
5.3 Affordability
Script: 04_affordability.R, 04_mobility_costs_money_car.R, 04_mobility_costs_money_pt.R, 04_mobility_costs_money_pt_cost_structure.R
Affordability is assessed by comparing transport and housing costs against household income. Commuting costs are computed for both car and public transport using r5r::detailed_itineraries() on the full set of jittered IMOB OD pairs, regardless of the reported travel mode.

5.3.1 Car costs
Script: 04_mobility_costs_money_car.R
Car commuting cost per trip = distance cost + tolls:
\[C_{car} = d \times 0.40\text{ €/km} + C_{toll}\]
The fixed cost of 0.40 €/km is based on Portaria n.º 1553-D/2008 de 31 de dezembro, which sets mileage allowance covering fuel and maintenance.
Tolls are computed via the Infraestruturas de Portugal toll API using the route geometry returned by r5r::detailed_itineraries().


5.3.2 Public transport costs — Two scenarios
Scripts: 04_mobility_costs_money_pt.R, 04_mobility_costs_money_pt_cost_structure.R
To account for uncertainty in PT fare payment practices, two cost scenarios are defined:
Scenario A — Single fare (pre-Navegante baseline):
Based on Carris Lisboa revenue data:

Total single ticket validations: 17,424,000
Total single ticket revenue: 33,278,000 €
Average cost per single ticket: ~1.91 €

Free transfers within the same mode for 60 minutes are assumed. Different-mode transfers incur an additional charge.
Scenario B — Monthly pass (Navegante):
Based on operator revenue data for monthly passes:



Pass type
Volume (months)
Price




Navegante Metropolitano
5,063,510
40 €


Navegante Municipal
887,190
30 €

1,103,782

243,784

3,425,013
0 €



Average monthly cost paid per pass holder: ~25.25 €
Assuming 44 journeys/month (2 trips × 22 working days): ~0.57 €/trip


5.3.3 Composite affordability indicators
Transport expenditure is expressed as a share of household income, with two variants (with and without housing costs):
\[\text{transp\_inc}_{car} = \frac{C_{car,year}}{I_{hh}}\]
\[\text{h\_transp\_inc}_{car} = \frac{C_{car,year} + C_{housing,year}}{I_{hh}}\]
A modal-share-weighted composite indicator combines car and PT costs proportionally to Census 2021 modal shares:
\[\text{transp\_inc\_comp} = \frac{C_{car} \cdot s_{car} + C_{PT} \cdot s_{PT}}{s_{car} + s_{PT} + s_{active}}\]
where \(s_m\) is the modal share of mode \(m\) from Census 2021.
Car costs are additionally adjusted for vehicle occupancy rate (from IMOB 2017, Table IV.7) and household size and mobile population fraction (from IMOB 2017, Table II.1), to convert per-trip costs to a household-level annual burden.
Income data: household income per parish from INE (Rendimento agregado 2023). https://www.ine.pt/xurl/pub/66303599
Housing costs: median monthly housing cost per parish from INE (Habitação custos 2021). https://www.ine.pt/xurl/pub/65586079






Canha parish



The parish of Canha (dtmnfr = 150701) is not served by any GTFS operator, resulting in NA PT costs. These values are imputed using the mean of neighbouring parishes within the same municipality (Montijo).

title: 5  Dimension Calculations
text:
5.4 Safety
Script: 04_safety.R
Source: ANSR — Autoridade Nacional de Segurança Rodoviária, road accident georeferenced point data (2019–2023).






flowchart TB
    Acc["ANSR Road Accidents<br/>2019–2023<br/>(georeferenced points)"] --> Filter
    Pop["Census Population<br/>(parish and grid)"] --> JoinPop
    Geo["Geographic units<br/>(parishes, municipalities, H3 grid)"] --> SJoin
    
    Filter["Filter: last 5 years<br/>(2019–2023)"] --> SJoin
    SJoin["st_join: assign accidents<br/>to geographic units"] --> ExcludeOuter["Exclude accidents<br/>outside built-up areas<br/>(ponts/bridges)"]
    ExcludeOuter --> Aggregate
    
    subgraph Aggregate["Aggregate per geographic unit"]
        direction TB
        TotalAcc["Total accidents (5 years)"] 
        Victims["Victim counts:<br/>fatal, serious, slight (30-day)"]
        Vehicles["Vehicles involved:<br/>motorised, cycle, pedestrian"]
        Context["Context flags:<br/>at night, within localities"]
    end
    
    Aggregate --> JoinPop["Join census population"] --> CalcIdx
    
    subgraph CalcIdx["Compute indexes"]
        direction TB
        SevIdx["Severity index:<br/>fatalities / total victims"]
        ModeIdx["Mode-specific severity:<br/>fatalities / vehicles involved (per mode)"]
        Rate1000["Accident rate per 1,000 residents"]
        FatalRate["Fatality rate per 1,000 residents"]
    end
    
    CalcIdx --> BothSets["All accidents<br/>+<br/>Within-localities only"]




Figure 5.3: Safety indicators computation






5.4.1 Accident data filtering
Accidents located outside administrative parish boundaries are excluded. This includes accidents on major bridges (Ponte 25 de Abril, Ponte Vasco da Gama) which cannot be assigned to a parish. Only accidents reported as occurring within localities (dentro das localidades) are used for the IMPT normalisation.


5.4.2 Safety indicators computed
For each geographical unit and transport mode:



Table 5.2: Safety indicators









Indicator
Formula




Total accidents (5 years)
Count


Fatalities (30-day)
Sum VM_30d


Serious injuries (30-day)
Sum FG_30d


Slight injuries (30-day)
Sum FL_30d


Accident rate
total_acidentes / population × 1000


Fatality rate
vitimas_mortais30 / population × 1000


Severity index (total)
fatalities / total_victims


Severity index (motorised)
fatalities / motorised_vehicles_involved


Severity index (cycling)
fatalities / cycling_involved


Severity index (pedestrian)
fatalities / pedestrians_involved










Pereira, Rafael H. M., and Daniel Herszenhut. 2022. Accessibility: Transport Accessibility Measures. https://ipeagit.github.io/accessibility/.

title: 6  Index Computation — IMPT Calculator
text:
6.1 Normalisation
Script: 05_IMPTcalculator.R
Package: FactoMineR (PCA)
All indicators are normalised to the range [1, 100] using min-max scaling:
Benefit indicators (higher raw value → better): \[X_{norm} = 1 + \frac{X - X_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}} \times 99\]
Cost indicators (lower raw value → better): \[X_{norm} = 1 + \frac{X_{max} - X}{X_{max} - X_{min}} \times 99\]
Accessibility metrics (access_*) are benefit indicators; mobility cost metrics (mobility_cost_*), PT waiting times, and all affordability indicators are cost indicators.
Missing values in accessibility indicators (arising from infeasible PT routes) are imputed with the mean of the respective variable prior to PCA.

title: 6  Index Computation — IMPT Calculator
text:
6.2 PCA-Based Dimension Scores
For each dimension (Accessibility, Mobility, Safety) and at the global (all-mode) and per-mode levels, a PCA is run using FactoMineR::PCA() with dtmnfr (parish ID) as a supplementary qualitative variable.
The first principal component (PC1) score is used as the dimension index, after rescaling to [1, 100]. PC1 captures the dominant axis of variation among the normalised indicators.
For Affordability, PCA is not applied (too few indicators); instead, the normalised composite h_transp_inc_comp (housing + transport cost as share of income, modal-share weighted) is used directly as the Affordability Index.
For Safety per mode, an equal-weight average of the severity index (inverted) and frequency (vehicle involvement count) is used instead of PCA.

title: 6  Index Computation — IMPT Calculator
text:
6.3 Composite IMPT Scores
Three aggregation methods are computed for the global IMPT and each per-mode IMPT:

Geometric mean (equal weights): \[IMPT_{geom} = \left(\prod_{d} (S_d + 1)\right)^{1/n} - 1\]
Arithmetic mean (equal weights): \[IMPT_{avg} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_d S_d\]
Entropy-weighted geometric mean: \[IMPT_{entropy} = \prod_d (S_d + 1)^{w_d} - 1\]

where weights \(w_d\) are derived from the entropy weighting method:
\[w_d = \frac{d_d}{\sum_j d_j}, \quad d_d = 1 - e_d, \quad e_d = -k \sum_i p_{id} \ln(p_{id})\]
with \(k = 1/\ln(n)\) and \(p_{id}\) being the proportion of the dimension score for area \(i\).

title: 6  Index Computation — IMPT Calculator
text:
6.4 Spatial Aggregation
Once scores are computed at parish level, they are aggregated to higher levels:

Municipality level: population-weighted mean of parish scores.
Grid level: parishes are assigned to grid cells via the grid_nuts.csv cross-reference table; scores are assigned from the host parish (no further averaging at this stage).

title: 7  Output and Dashboard
text:
7.1 Output Files
Final results are stored in three geographic formats for each spatial level:
Results are uploaded to the project data server at https://impt.server.ushift.pt, to the ushift server at https://ushift.tecnico.ulisboa.pt/content/impt/, and to the GitHub release using piggyback.

title: 7  Output and Dashboard
text:
Table 7.1: Output file formats










Level
Format
Variables




Parish (freguesia)
CSV + GeoPackage
All dimension scores + IMPT scores


Municipality (município)
CSV + GeoPackage
Population-weighted means


H3 Grid
CSV + GeoPackage
Assigned from host parish

title: 7  Output and Dashboard
text:
7.2 Dashboard Export
Script: 06b_dashboard_export.R
The dashboard export script joins all indicator tables (accessibility, mobility, affordability, safety, census/land use, IMPT scores) with the spatial geometry files and exports them as three aggregated GeoJSON files for direct use in the web dashboard:

grid_aggregated.geojson
freguesias_aggregated.geojson
municipios_aggregated.geojson

A champions lookup file (champions.json) is also exported, identifying the top contributing indicators to each dimension’s PC1, for display in the dashboard sidebar.

title: 7  Output and Dashboard
text:
7.3 Web Dashboard
The IMPT results are visualised in an interactive web dashboard built with React + TypeScript + Vite, using Leaflet/MapLibre for map rendering. The dashboard is available at:

ushift.pt/apps/impt

The dashboard supports:

Map visualisation by metric, mode, and geographic level
Metric cards with legend, description, and top contributor indicators
Dynamic IMPT score computation with user-defined dimension weights
PT fare scenario switching (single fare vs. Navegante pass)
Download results and indicators as CSV and GeoJSON

title: 8  Reproducibility
text:
8.1 Code Repository
All data preparation and analysis scripts are available in the IMPT-data GitHub repository:
Scripts are organised with a numeric prefix indicating execution order:

title: 8  Reproducibility
text:
https://github.com/U-Shift/IMPT-data





Table 8.1: Pipeline script organisation










Prefix
Phase
Script(s)




00
Setup
00a_impt_data_handle.R, 00b_data_load.R


01
Geometry & base data preparation
01_data_prep.R


02
Socio-demographics & land use
02_*.R (5 scripts)


03
Routing & travel time matrices
03_ttm_gridh3.R


04
Dimension calculations
04_*.R (10 scripts)


05
Index computation
05_IMPTcalculator.R, 05a_results_export_pca.R


06
Output & dashboard
06a_results_load.R, 06b_dashboard_export.R

title: 8  Reproducibility
text:
8.2 Replication for Other Areas
To replicate the IMPT for another Portuguese metropolitan area:

Replace the CAOP 2024 filter (NUTS-II codes "Grande Lisboa" and "Península de Setúbal") with the target region.
Update the GTFS operator list in 01_data_prep.R.
Obtain the IMOB travel survey OD matrix for the target area, and apply the dicofre conversion if needed.
Set up a HOT Export Tool bounding box for the new area.
Download the relevant Copernicus DEM tile.
Run scripts in order (00 → 06).

The H3 grid generation, routing engine, and scoring methodology are entirely generic and require no structural changes.
